errors: ; The age upper bound uses a strict “Less than 24” check, which excludes patients who are exactly 24 years old. Most chlamydia screening guidelines include women up to and including age 24 (≤ 24).; The value set for “Reason for not performing Chlamydia Screening” is listed as “TBD” and has no defined identifier or contents, so the rule cannot correctly identify documented reasons for not screening.; The gender check references a value set “Female Administrative Sex” from the Common library; if that value set does not contain the appropriate SNOMED or administrative gender codes, the gender filter could fail. (This depends on the Common library definition.)

; The look‑back period for the mammogram is set to **1 year**, but most breast‑cancer screening guidelines (e.g., USPSTF, ACS) recommend a **2‑year** interval for average‑risk women aged 50‑74 (and optional annual screening for women 40‑49). The 1‑year interval is therefore not clinically aligned with standard recommendations.; The **Mammogram** definition uses `IsNull( ProcedureLookBack(...) )`, which evaluates to **true when no mammogram is found** in the look‑back window. Consequently, the `MeetsInclusionCriteria` will be true only when the patient **has NOT had a mammogram** in the past year, leading to a recommendation to “Perform mammogram screening” for patients who are already overdue, rather than confirming they have been screened. This logic is inverted and clinically incorrect.; Because the inclusion criteria are based on the inverted mammogram check, the overall recommendation logic does not reflect the intended clinical guideline (i.e., recommend screening for women ≥ 40 years who have not been screened within the appropriate interval). The values and expressions in the ELM therefore do not represent a clinically reasonable implementation.

The HbA1c threshold used for the “HbA1c Laboratory Test” expression is set to ≥ 9 g% (≈ 9 %). Clinical guidelines (e.g., ADA, WHO) define diabetes diagnosis at an HbA1c ≥ 6.5 % (or ≥ 48 mmol/mol). Using 9 % as the diagnostic cut‑off is clinically unreasonable for identifying a new diabetes diagnosis; it would miss many patients who meet standard criteria. The age criterion (≥ 18 years) is reasonable.

; The CDS logic checks for any active “Tobacco User” Condition but does **not** verify the required 20‑pack‑year smoking history that USPSTF recommends for lung‑cancer screening.; The logic also omits the USPSTF criterion that the patient must have **quit smoking ≤15 years ago** (or be a current smoker). Without this, former smokers who quit >15 years ago could be incorrectly flagged.; Because the smoking‑history check is based solely on the presence of a Condition in the “Tobacco User” value set, it may miss patients whose smoking exposure is recorded in other ways (e.g., Observation of pack‑year). This makes the implementation less clinically accurate.; All other criteria (age 50‑80, no LDCT in the past year) are reasonable, but the missing pack‑year and quit‑time requirements render the overall CDS not clinically aligned with the USPSTF guideline.

; **Age range**: The CDS limits inclusion to patients aged 60‑75 years. Current USPSTF and other major guidelines recommend colorectal cancer screening for average‑risk adults starting at age 45 (or 50 in older guidelines). Excluding patients aged 45‑59 omits a large portion of the guideline‑eligible population.; **Screening interval**: The rule treats any colorectal‑cancer‑screening observation within the past **2 years** as “recent.” This is appropriate for annual FIT or stool DNA tests, but not for colonoscopy (recommended every 10 years) or flexible sigmoidoscopy (every 5 years). Using a 2‑year window could incorrectly flag patients who had a colonoscopy 5‑10 years ago as needing repeat screening.; **Observation code set**: The value set “Colorectal Cancer Screening” is referenced, but the ELM does not differentiate between modality‑specific codes (e.g., colonoscopy vs. FIT). Without that distinction, the 2‑year rule cannot be applied correctly to each modality.; **Rationale expression**: The `Rationale` element always returns `Null` regardless of whether the patient meets criteria, providing no explanatory text for the recommendation.; **Error handling**: The `Errors` element is hard‑coded to `Null`, so any data‑quality issues (missing birthDate, no observations, etc.) will not be reported.

; **Age criterion** – The logic uses a minimum age of 30 years (`In Demographic`), but most HIV screening guidelines (e.g., CDC, USPSTF) recommend routine screening for all individuals starting at age 13–15 (or up to 64 years). The 30‑year cutoff is not clinically aligned with standard recommendations.; **Frequency of testing** – The rule requires a “recent” HIV test defined as any test performed within the past 1 year. Current guidelines advise a one‑time screening for low‑risk adults and annual testing only for persons at increased risk. Requiring a test within the last year for everyone ≥30 years may miss patients who were screened appropriately more than a year ago.; **Risk assessment** – The model does not incorporate known risk factors (e.g., sexual behavior, injection drug use, pregnancy) that influence the recommended testing interval. A purely age‑based rule without risk stratification is incomplete relative to clinical practice.; **Rationale field** – The `Rationale` expression always returns `null`, providing no explanatory text for the recommendation. Clinical decision support artifacts typically include a rationale to help clinicians understand why the recommendation is generated.

; **BMI threshold** – The CDS uses a cutoff of **BMI ≥ 35 kg/m²** to define obesity. Clinical guidelines (e.g., USPSTF, ADA) define the screening population as adults who are **overweight or obese (BMI ≥ 25 kg/m²)**. The current threshold is too high and would miss many patients who meet guideline criteria.; **Screening interval** – The rule requires a **glucose/HbA1c test within the past 1 year** to consider the patient up‑to‑date. USPSTF recommends screening **every 3 years** for the target age/weight group (more frequent testing may be appropriate for higher‑risk individuals, but a 1‑year interval is stricter than the guideline baseline). This interval does not align with the standard recommendation.

; **Age range mismatch** – The decision logic (`In Demographic`) restricts screening to women ≥ 30 years old, yet the USPSTF recommendation cited in the `Rationale` (and current guidelines) apply to women **21 – 65 years**. This creates an inconsistency between the implemented rule and the stated clinical guideline.; **Guideline consistency** – Because the rationale states “women aged 21‑65 years,” the lower age bound should be 21, not 30.; **Optional screening modality** – Current USPSTF guidance also allows HPV testing (or co‑testing) every 5 years for women 30‑65. The CDS only recommends Pap cytology every 3 years, which is acceptable but omits the alternative recommended option; this should be noted if the intent is to fully reflect the guideline.

; The ELM requires a “recent” depression screening to be within the past **3 months**. USPSTF guidance recommends **annual** screening (or at least once per year), not a 3‑month interval, making the timing more restrictive than the guideline.; The logic **excludes patients with a bipolar diagnosis** from screening. USPSTF does not specify exclusion of bipolar disorder; patients with bipolar may still benefit from depression screening, so this exclusion is not aligned with the guideline.; The rationale text states “screening for depression in the general adult population (age 12+)”. Age 12+ actually includes adolescents, and the wording should reflect that the recommendation covers both adults and adolescents per USPSTF. This minor inconsistency does not affect the core logic but indicates a mismatch in phrasing.

The CDS uses an age threshold of ≥ 75 years (In Demographic) to identify patients who need falls‑prevention screening, but the USPSTF recommendation cited in the Rationale applies to community‑dwelling adults ≥ 65 years old. This creates a clinical inconsistency: patients aged 65‑74 who meet the guideline criteria would be excluded by the CDS logic. The age cutoff should be aligned with the guideline (≥ 65 years) to be clinically reasonable.

The age criterion is inconsistent with the cited guideline. The USPSTF recommends alcohol misuse screening for adults ≥ 18 years, and the rationale text reflects this, but the ELM logic (“In Demographic”) requires patients to be ≥ 25 years old. This mismatch makes the CDS recommendation clinically unreasonable for the intended population.

The age eligibility criteria are inconsistent with the USPSTF recommendation referenced in the rationale. The ELM defines “In Demographic” as patients aged 55 to 75 years, while the USPSTF guideline (and the rationale text) specifies screening for men aged 65 to 75 years who have ever smoked. This lower age bound mismatch makes the CDS logic clinically unreasonable.

; Inclusion criteria uses an **OR** between “Screening Age” (age ≥ 40) and “Gender” (female). Clinical guidelines for breast‑cancer screening require **both** conditions (female **and** age ≥ 40) to be met; the logic should be an **AND**, not an OR.; The age threshold is set to **40 years**. Many current U.S. guidelines (e.g., USPSTF) recommend routine screening beginning at **50 years** (or 40 years only for higher‑risk groups). The age cutoff does not match the referenced guideline.; The look‑back interval for the mammogram is defined as **1 year**. Standard recommendations often use a **2‑year** interval for women aged 40‑49 and a **1‑year** interval for women ≥ 50. The single 1‑year look‑back does not reflect the guideline’s age‑specific interval.; The “Mammogram” definition checks `IsNull(ProcedureLookBack(...))`, which returns **true** when no mammogram is found. While this aligns with “recommend if not done,” the naming is misleading and could be confused with a positive result. The expression should be renamed or clarified.

The CDS logic recommends tobacco use screening for all patients age ≥ 18 years (In Demographic) without checking whether a recent screening has already been performed. The definition of **Needs Tobacco Use Screening** should also require that **Has Recent Tobacco Screening** is false (e.g., `In Demographic AND NOT Has Recent Tobacco Screening`). As written, the recommendation will fire even for patients who have a valid tobacco use observation within the past year, which is not clinically reasonable according to USPSTF guidance.

; The decision logic checks only that the patient is ≥ 50 years old; it does **not** enforce the upper age limit of 80 years recommended by USPSTF.; “Has Smoking History” merely looks for any active Condition coded in the “Tobacco User” value set. It does **not** verify the required ≥ 20 pack‑year smoking exposure (or the 15‑year quit window) that the USPSTF guideline specifies. Consequently the rationale text (“20 pack‑year smoking history”) is inconsistent with the actual criteria.; The guideline also limits screening to adults who have smoked within the past 15 years; this temporal quit‑date requirement is absent from the ELM.; Using an Observation with the “Low Dose CT Chest” value set to represent a prior LDCT may be acceptable, but LDCT is typically captured as a Procedure (ImagingStudy) rather than an Observation; this could lead to missed detections of prior scans.

; **Bipolar exclusion not applied** – the ELM defines a “Has Bipolar Diagnosis” expression but never uses it to exclude patients with bipolar disorder from depression screening, contrary to most clinical guidelines that recommend separate assessment pathways for bipolar patients.; **Age‑specific tool selection not enforced** – the recommendation text mentions using PHQ‑9 for adults and PHQ‑A for adolescents, yet the decision logic does not differentiate between adult (≥18 y) and adolescent (12‑17 y) patients when determining the need for screening or when selecting the appropriate observation value set. This could lead to inappropriate tool use.; **Pregnancy/post‑partum not explicitly considered** – USPSTF specifically highlights pregnant and postpartum persons; while they are captured by the ≥12 y age rule, the logic does not ensure that pregnancy status is evaluated, which may be required for certain implementation contexts.

; **Look‑back period for colonoscopy** is set to **1 month** (`Quantity` value = 1, unit = "month") which is clinically unreasonable. Current colorectal cancer screening guidelines recommend a **10‑year** interval (or 5 years for flexible sigmoidoscopy) after a normal colonoscopy, not a 1‑month window.; **Inclusion logic** requires a colonoscopy to have occurred within the look‑back period (`Colonoscopy` is true when the count of colonoscopy procedures in the look‑back window > 0). This is the opposite of typical screening criteria, which usually require **no colonoscopy** in the past 10 years to be eligible for a new screening.; **Age threshold** is set to **≥ 50 years**. While many older guidelines used 50 years, the most recent USPSTF recommendation lowers the starting age to **45 years**. Using 50 years may miss eligible patients aged 45‑49.

The logic for determining “Needs Falls Prevention Screening” is clinically inverted. The CDS currently requires a recent falls risk assessment (Has Recent Falls Assessment) to trigger a recommendation for screening, whereas the guideline calls for screening **when a recent assessment is NOT present** (i.e., patients aged ≥ 65 years who have not had a falls risk assessment in the past year). The condition should be the negation of the Exists query (or the query should check for the absence of a recent assessment). This mismatch makes the recommendation inappropriate for the intended population.

The exclusion criteria for “Needs Depression Screening” are not clinically aligned with USPSTF guidance.; The expression `Not (Has Bipolar Diagnosis AND Has Depression Diagnosis)` only excludes patients who have **both** a bipolar and a depression diagnosis. Clinically, patients should be excluded from routine depression screening if they have **either** a bipolar disorder (because depressive symptoms may be part of bipolar illness) **or** an existing depression diagnosis (since they are already identified as depressed).; Therefore the logical condition should be `Not (Has Bipolar Diagnosis OR Has Depression Diagnosis)` (or separate NOT checks for each diagnosis) to correctly reflect the guideline. This mismatch makes the CDS recommendation potentially inappropriate for patients with a single diagnosis.
